description:
You are a Linux systems engineer who has just finished tightening several
    configuration files.  
    A quick integrity-preserving backup is required so that the Security
    Operations team can restore the hardened files at any time.

    PRE-EXISTING DATA (already present on the VM):
       • Directory: /home/user/hardening
            ├── sshd_config
            └── sysctl.conf

    Goal:
       1. Compress the entire /home/user/hardening directory into a single
          gzip-compressed tar archive stored in:
              /home/user/archives/hardening_backup.tar.gz
          Create /home/user/archives/ first if it does not exist.
       2. Immediately test the archive by extracting it to:
              /home/user/restore_test
       3. Produce a plain-text logfile named
              /home/user/compression_report.log
          containing EXACTLY the following four newline-separated key=value
          pairs (no extra spaces, no blank lines, no surrounding quotes):

              ARCHIVE_PATH=/home/user/archives/hardening_backup.tar.gz
              FILES_IN_ARCHIVE=2
              RESTORE_DIR=/home/user/restore_test
              HASH_MATCH=OK

          • FILES_IN_ARCHIVE must be the count of regular files contained
            inside the archive (should be “2” for the two configs).
          • HASH_MATCH must be “OK” only if the SHA-256 hash of each original
            file exactly matches the hash of its counterpart in
            /home/user/restore_test.  If they do not match, write “FAIL”.

    Constraints & Tips:
       • Perform every action using normal user permissions (no sudo).
       • Use only typical GNU/Linux command-line utilities that ship with most
         distros (tar, mkdir, sha256sum, etc.).
       • The automated checker will validate:
           – Archive existence and location
           – Correct extraction path and file presence
           – That both restored files are byte-for-byte identical to the
             originals
           – That /home/user/compression_report.log exists and the four lines
             are identical to the specification above (including values).
       • Do NOT alter the original /home/user/hardening directory contents.
truth:
Pre-existing directory structure and file contents before the agent starts:

    /home/user/hardening/
        ├── sshd_config
        │     Content (exactly two lines, trailing LF on each):
        │         PasswordAuthentication no\n
        │         PermitRootLogin no\n
        └── sysctl.conf
              Content (exactly two lines, trailing LF on each):
                  net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0\n
                  kernel.randomize_va_space = 2\n

    The judge will compute SHA-256 hashes of both original files, then compare
    them with the hashes of the files extracted to /home/user/restore_test/.
    If both pairs match, HASH_MATCH in /home/user/compression_report.log must
    read “OK”; otherwise, “FAIL”.

    Expected log file path and absolute content for a correct solution:

    /home/user/compression_report.log
        ARCHIVE_PATH=/home/user/archives/hardening_backup.tar.gz\n
        FILES_IN_ARCHIVE=2\n
        RESTORE_DIR=/home/user/restore_test\n
        HASH_MATCH=OK\n
